content:
Hi everyone!
Noticed that there is no support yet for WARN_ONCE, so I tested out an implementation that works exactly like the "lite" variant in the kernel using a static bool:

macro_rules! do_once_lite_if {
    ($cond: expr, $e: expr) => {
        #[link_section = ".data.once"]
        static mut __ALREADY_DONE: bool = false;
        if unlikely($cond && unsafe { !__ALREADY_DONE }) {
            unsafe { __ALREADY_DONE = true; }
            $e;
        }
    }
}

macro_rules! warn_once {
    ($($arg:tt)*) => (
        do_once_lite_if!(true, crate::pr_warn!($($arg)*))
    )
}

This seems to work but I was wondering if this kind of unsafe usage is ok? Worst case would be that the do once part is done nproc times instead of once; but that's also true for the C version.

2nd question: How do you replace unlikely? I've read that #[cold] can be used to achieve a similar effect, but I don't really see how?
Using #[cold] requires a separate function. There's no direct equivalent to unlikely without unstable features.
Your code doesn't guarantee that it runs exactly once. It could run twice if two threads hit it in parallel.
I guess that's not so bad since the only consequence is a WARN_ONCE printing twice.
And we shouldn't use it for anything but WARN_ONCE since anything in .data.once is affected by clear_warn_once_set.
I would probably prefer to see this using atomic or at least volatile reads and writes.

Alice Ryhl said:

Your code doesn't guarantee that it runs exactly once. It could run twice if two threads hit it in parallel.

Or even more often in a pathological case. But as you wrote, it's "just" logging and I think it's the only way to approach the performance of the C version, which also suffers from this behavior. (see once_lite.h ).

Jens Korinth said:

This seems to work but I was wondering if this kind of unsafe usage is ok? Worst case would be that the do once part is done nproc times instead of once; but that's also true for the C version.

the unsafe code is unsound, if two threads/cpus simultaneously enter the if statement, they will both write to __ALREADY_DONE, which is UB.

Alice Ryhl said:

I would probably prefer to see this using atomic or at least volatile reads and writes.

The "heavy" version uses static key patching, but I'm nowhere proficient enough to emulate that in Rust. :blush: Both volatile and atomic would have a prohibitive impact on performance, I think. Also, bool reads are atomic anyhow, are they not?

Jens Korinth said:

Both volatile and atomic would have a prohibitive impact on performance, I think.

Did you measure that? IIRC relaxed atomic accesses are just normal mov instructions on x86, so they should have the same performance as a normal access (except for compiler optimization probably).

Jens Korinth said:

Also, bool reads are atomic anyhow, are they not?

What do you mean? A normal boolean access is not atomic.

Benno Lossin said:

Jens Korinth said:

Both volatile and atomic would have a prohibitive impact on performance, I think.

Did you measure that? IIRC relaxed atomic accesses are just normal mov instructions on x86, so they should have the same performance as a normal access (except for compiler optimization probably).

Jens Korinth said:

Also, bool reads are atomic anyhow, are they not?

What do you mean? A normal boolean access is not atomic.

They are not? I thought C11 guarantees that a bool is always using the native bitwidth of the architecture, which makes reads atomic. Didn’t realize that isn’t true in the kernel.
I am purely talking about Rust here
it might be that in practice they are, but the code above still is considered UB when two threads concurrently write to the static

Benno Lossin said:

Jens Korinth said:

This seems to work but I was wondering if this kind of unsafe usage is ok? Worst case would be that the do once part is done nproc times instead of once; but that's also true for the C version.

the unsafe code is unsound, if two threads/cpus simultaneously enter the if statement, they will both write to __ALREADY_DONE, which is UB.

You are right. I would argue that it is a) the same as in the C version, b) preferable to any performance impact, and c) simultaneous writes would at least always agree on the value.
it's still UB
did you measure the performance impact?

Benno Lossin said:

did you measure the performance impact?

Will do, I’m curious, too.

Jens Korinth said:

Alice Ryhl said:

I would probably prefer to see this using atomic or at least volatile reads and writes.

The "heavy" version uses static key patching, but I'm nowhere proficient enough to emulate that in Rust. :blush: Both volatile and atomic would have a prohibitive impact on performance, I think. Also, bool reads are atomic anyhow, are they not?

Why do you think they would have a prohibitive impact? Both volatile and relaxed atomics compile down to ordinary mov instructions. The only difference is the compiler's ability to perform optimizations, and I don't see what optimization that would be. In fact, you mostly only lose optimizations that would be incorrect!
(For relaxed atomics I'm only talking about loads and stores. If you do a cmpxchg of course that's a different story, but that's not what I'm suggesting.)
As for emulating static key patching, I've already done it for you: [PATCH v11 0/5] Tracepoints and static branch in Rust

Alice Ryhl said:

Jens Korinth said:

Alice Ryhl said:

I would probably prefer to see this using atomic or at least volatile reads and writes.

The "heavy" version uses static key patching, but I'm nowhere proficient enough to emulate that in Rust. :blush: Both volatile and atomic would have a prohibitive impact on performance, I think. Also, bool reads are atomic anyhow, are they not?

Why do you think they would have a prohibitive impact? Both volatile and relaxed atomics compile down to ordinary mov instructions. The only difference is the compiler's ability to perform optimizations, and I don't see what optimization that would be. In fact, you mostly only lose optimizations that would be incorrect!

Volatile means no cache - every read and every write has to be performed on actual memory, which is at least an order of magnitude slower.

Whether or not atomics compile down to mov instructions depends entirely on the ISA. If there are no viable instructions to do it, the compiler will have to construct a sound alternative. I guess that would mean barriers/locking. This is not an issue on x86_64, but Linux runs on a lot of very different platforms.
Volatile certainly does not skip CPU caches.
As for atomics, other than 64-bit atomics loads on some 32-bit archs being made stronger to avoid tearing, I'm not aware of any such examples.
I guess the bigger issue with atomics is that the kernel doesn't have boolean sized atomics

I thought C11 guarantees that a bool is always using the native bitwidth of the architecture, which makes reads atomic.

I am not sure what you mean by that, but C11's _Bool is typically 1 byte (not word size), can hold only 0 or 1, and reading it is not atomic (and something being word sized does not make anything atomic either).

Alice Ryhl said:

Volatile certainly does not skip CPU caches.

You're absolutely right, another thing I've learned today. :flushed:

Alice Ryhl said:

I guess the bigger issue with atomics is that the kernel doesn't have boolean sized atomics

Well, any int type will do, right? Or are you concerned about the size?

Miguel Ojeda said:

I thought C11 guarantees that a bool is always using the native bitwidth of the architecture, which makes reads atomic.

I am not sure what you mean by that, but C11's _Bool is typically 1 byte (not word size), can hold only 0 or 1, and reading it is not atomic (and something being word sized does not make anything atomic either).

That's also true. I've wrongly assumed that true and false would be _Bool-sized, but apparently they are not?! :face_with_peeking_eye: But for my own sanity: word-sized reads and writes are always atomic, right?
In assembly language, yes.
But the C/Rust compiler is allowed to split a single write into two. It's also allowed to perform a read twice and generate code that is incorrect if the two reads return different values.
Interesting, learned a lot already, thanks for your kind answers! I will play around a bit with different approaches and come back with some numbers.

Alice Ryhl said:

But the C/Rust compiler is allowed to split a single write into two. It's also allowed to perform a read twice and generate code that is incorrect if the two reads return different values.

Within one week two times the same explanation. With nearly the same wording :big_smile:

What would be a good place to document this?

I think it is documented: https://docs.kernel.org/core-api/wrappers/memory-barriers.html (as well as others like https://docs.kernel.org/process/volatile-considered-harmful.html and https://docs.kernel.org/core-api/wrappers/atomic_t.html)

Of course, sometimes it is hard to know what is documented.

@Benno Lossin @Alice Ryhl Tried to setup a benchmark resembling the situation which evaluates:

  1. the initial UB mut bool approach,
  2. mut bool with read_volatile/write_volatile,
  3. AtomicBool with Ordering::Relaxed,
  4. AtomicBool with Ordering::SeqCst,
  5. RwLock (for good measure, it's clear it won't perform well here).

Code is on my github - if you can find the time, pls check if it makes sense to you or if there are any mistakes which poison the results.

Results on my machine (Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-8365U CPU @ 1.60GHz):

Benchmarking mut_bool: Warming up for 3.0000 scomplex 42 arguments are void
mut_bool                time:   [1.2260 ns 1.2283 ns 1.2312 ns]
                        change: [-0.8516% -0.1852% +0.4157%] (p = 0.59 > 0.05)
                        No change in performance detected.
Found 17 outliers among 100 measurements (17.00%)
  17 (17.00%) high severe

Benchmarking mut_volatile_bool: Warming up for 3.0000 scomplex 42 arguments are void
mut_volatile_bool       time:   [245.27 µs 245.90 µs 246.78 µs]
                        change: [-0.7416% +0.1784% +1.0277%] (p = 0.71 > 0.05)
                        No change in performance detected.
Found 21 outliers among 100 measurements (21.00%)
  3 (3.00%) high mild
  18 (18.00%) high severe

Benchmarking abool_relaxed: Warming up for 3.0000 scomplex 42 arguments are void
abool_relaxed           time:   [246.03 µs 246.93 µs 248.01 µs]
                        change: [-0.2734% +0.3719% +0.9428%] (p = 0.28 > 0.05)
                        No change in performance detected.
Found 14 outliers among 100 measurements (14.00%)
  2 (2.00%) high mild
  12 (12.00%) high severe

Benchmarking abool_seqcst: Warming up for 3.0000 scomplex 42 arguments are void
abool_seqcst            time:   [246.30 µs 246.97 µs 247.79 µs]
                        change: [-10.562% -8.3117% -6.3426%] (p = 0.00 < 0.05)
                        Performance has improved.
Found 13 outliers among 100 measurements (13.00%)
  3 (3.00%) high mild
  10 (10.00%) high severe

Benchmarking rwlock: Warming up for 3.0000 scomplex 42 arguments are void
rwlock                  time:   [11.777 ms 11.790 ms 11.805 ms]
                        change: [-2.4501% -1.9311% -1.4223%] (p = 0.00 < 0.05)
                        Performance has improved.
Found 13 outliers among 100 measurements (13.00%)
  4 (4.00%) high mild
  9 (9.00%) high severe
With mut_bool, the loop gets optimized away.

After the first iteration, the compiler knows that the boolean must be true. Either it started out true, or we just set it to true. And since you're using unsynchronized loads, the compiler knows that other threads can't modify the value in parallel, so there's no way it could have changed. Thus, reading the global must return true, skipping the loop. Same logic applies to iteration 3 and 4 and so on.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I think it is documented: https://docs.kernel.org/core-api/wrappers/memory-barriers.html (as well as others like https://docs.kernel.org/process/volatile-considered-harmful.html and https://docs.kernel.org/core-api/wrappers/atomic_t.html)

Of course, sometimes it is hard to know what is documented.

Another learning material is the Rust Atomics and Locks book, for example, Borrowing and Data Races
This should be a MUST-read book for developing kernel code in Rust.

Alice Ryhl said:

With mut_bool, the loop gets optimized away.

It could go away, yeah. For whatever reason, a trivial loop remains that spins decrementing the counter in a register and does not do anything else.

Btw, the DO_ONCE_LITE() in C seems to be wrong/unsafe, because you could have two threads calling it at the same time, and both read __already_done as false, and then they would all call the function func, which makes it "do-it-twice".

@Boqun Feng Yes, the C code has the same problem. Could be more than twice, too, up to the number of processor cores, I think.

I assumed that it was a conscious decision on the C side. Even the worst case is pretty benign, after all.

Miguel Ojeda said:

Alice Ryhl said:

With mut_bool, the loop gets optimized away.

It could go away, yeah. For whatever reason, a trivial loop remains that spins decrementing the counter in a register and does not do anything else.

Doesn’t that mean that the loop is still there and only the call is optimized away? Need to check the asm later. :thinking:
Sounds like a missed optimization :woman_shrugging:

True. But I’m not sure if the compiler can really prove anything about static muts. In a language with pointers it doesn’t seem to be sound to assume there will be no other places mutating this var. Which would explain why the loop is still there. The call might be skipped by branch prediction.

Using unsynchronized reads and writes are a promise to the compiler that there are no other places mutating the var. The compiler will optimize under that assumption, and if you read the assembly, it is doing so in this case.

The loop looks like this:

        mov     eax, 999999
.LBB0_3:
        dec     rax
        jne     .LBB0_3
        ret
There's clearly not touching the static here.
I don't know why it's not optimized away completely, but it should be clear that the compiler doesn't think it is important to touch the static in that loop.
could be that criteration use a blackbox for each iteration
No, I compiled it in CE without criterion.
It's literally just a for _ in 0..ITERATIONS
(And anyway criterion is outside the loop and there is no proc macro touching that etc.)

Alice Ryhl said:

It's literally just a for _ in 0..ITERATIONS

you mean the criterion code? But in the loop body there is a blackbox, no?
No.

const ITERATIONS: usize = 1000000;

#[link_section = ".data.once"]
static mut ALREADY_DONE: bool = false;

#[inline(never)]
fn println() {}

pub fn bench_mut_bool() {
    for _ in 0..ITERATIONS {
        if unsafe { !ALREADY_DONE } {
            unsafe { ALREADY_DONE = true; }
            println();
        }
    }
}

example::bench_mut_bool::h0d67ad6433122450:
        cmp     byte ptr [rip + example::ALREADY_DONE::h7b6dad1edb427cee], 0
        jne     .LBB0_2
        mov     byte ptr [rip + example::ALREADY_DONE::h7b6dad1edb427cee], 1
.LBB0_2:
        mov     eax, 999999
.LBB0_3:
        dec     rax
        jne     .LBB0_3
        ret

example::ALREADY_DONE::h7b6dad1edb427cee:
        .byte   0

Jens Korinth said:

Benno Lossin Alice Ryhl Tried to setup a benchmark resembling the situation which evaluates:

  1. the initial UB mut bool approach,
  2. mut bool with read_volatile/write_volatile,
  3. AtomicBool with Ordering::Relaxed,
  4. AtomicBool with Ordering::SeqCst,
  5. RwLock (for good measure, it's clear it won't perform well here).

Code is on my github - if you can find the time, pls check if it makes sense to you or if there are any mistakes which poison the results.

Results on my machine (Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-8365U CPU @ 1.60GHz):

Thanks for trying it out! IMHO, micro-benchmarks are often useless, since there are a lot of other factors when it comes to real-world applications. They might give you some insights into what is faster when designing for example a small algorithm. But they are often not precise and sometimes completely wrong when compared with benchmarks of a real application. One example of that is what was already discussed by Alice and Miguel, micro-benchmarks can often be optimized by the compiler into a trivial program.

On our team, we have got Andreas Hindborg who maintains the rust nvme driver as well as the rust block layer abstractions. He has a whole benchmark setup for the nvme driver to test the performance implications. Those results are far more reliable compared to any suite of mirco-benchmarks in my opinion.
If you (/your employer) have your own benchmark suite, it would be interesting to know how all of those solutions compare in your application.

Also, you executed your benchmark suite on x86_64, I didn't really expect a big difference between the volatile and two atomic approaches, since (with my very limited knowledge) under x86 all of them will probably be a mov instruction. Since you mentioned above that there are other architectures where that is not the case and where atomics are expensive, it would be great if you did the benchmarks there.

Finally, I am completely against any solution that uses UB (there are certain gray areas that I am willing to accept, but this is not one of them). We're trying to minimize the amount of memory bugs in the kernel using Rust. Relying on UB for performance, especially without real-world experience to back it up is not the right way. If you really, really need a more efficient way to set a global variable concurrently, I suggest you contact the Rust language developers. They might be able to add a new way to write to a variable that doesn't introduce UB, but is faster than any current way. Although you probably need very good motivation to get it in.
I do think it's quite telling that most of the solutions have exactly the same speed. The only exceptions is the one that got optimized away, and the one that uses a lock.
Yes, when I compiled all of them yesterday, those three in the middle compiled to essentially the same thing (the seqcst one with an extra xchg).

Benno Lossin said:

Jens Korinth said:

Benno Lossin Alice Ryhl Tried to setup a benchmark resembling the situation which evaluates:

  1. the initial UB mut bool approach,
  2. mut bool with read_volatile/write_volatile,
  3. AtomicBool with Ordering::Relaxed,
  4. AtomicBool with Ordering::SeqCst,
  5. RwLock (for good measure, it's clear it won't perform well here).

Code is on my github - if you can find the time, pls check if it makes sense to you or if there are any mistakes which poison the results.

Results on my machine (Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-8365U CPU @ 1.60GHz):

Thanks for trying it out! IMHO, micro-benchmarks are often useless, since there are a lot of other factors when it comes to real-world applications. They might give you some insights into what is faster when designing for example a small algorithm. But they are often not precise and sometimes completely wrong when compared with benchmarks of a real application. One example of that is what was already discussed by Alice and Miguel, micro-benchmarks can often be optimized by the compiler into a trivial program.

On our team, we have got Andreas Hindborg who maintains the rust nvme driver as well as the rust block layer abstractions. He has a whole benchmark setup for the nvme driver to test the performance implications. Those results are far more reliable compared to any suite of mirco-benchmarks in my opinion.
If you (/your employer) have your own benchmark suite, it would be interesting to know how all of those solutions compare in your application.

What exactly would you consider the "real application" here? This functionality may be used anywhere in the kernel, what exactly should be measured instead? And how? We're talking about milli- and microseconds here in a completely artificial example; in any real world scenario, say insmodding a .ko or something, it seems extremely unlikely that the runtime contribution of this code could even be measured with any degree of confidence?
For example the nvme driver. But it doesn't have to be that big to "count". As long as it is upstreamable, it qualifies.
It seemed to me that you have a usage for warn_once, so I thought that you might be able to just benchmark that

Jens Korinth said:

We're talking about milli- and microseconds here in a completely artificial example; in any real world scenario, say insmodding a .ko or something, it seems extremely unlikely that the runtime contribution of this code could even be measured with any degree of confidence?

If you can't even measure the difference, why do you even want to optimize this piece of code?

Benno Lossin said:

Jens Korinth said:

We're talking about milli- and microseconds here in a completely artificial example; in any real world scenario, say insmodding a .ko or something, it seems extremely unlikely that the runtime contribution of this code could even be measured with any degree of confidence?

If you can't even measure the difference, why do you even want to optimize this piece of code?

Well, there's a difference between things you cannot measure, and things that do not have impact. If this piece of code is used freely in a million different places it will have an impact on runtime performance. But you won't be able to detect it by looking at any one driver in isolation, because its contribution to runtime will be too small compared to the rest of the code (signal/noise).
I doubt that adding volatile will change the generated machine code in any real scenarios.

Jens Korinth said:

Benno Lossin said:

Jens Korinth said:

We're talking about milli- and microseconds here in a completely artificial example; in any real world scenario, say insmodding a .ko or something, it seems extremely unlikely that the runtime contribution of this code could even be measured with any degree of confidence?

If you can't even measure the difference, why do you even want to optimize this piece of code?

Well, there's a difference between things you cannot measure, and things that do not have impact. If this piece of code is used freely in a million different places it will have an impact on runtime performance. But you won't be able to detect it by looking at any one driver in isolation, because its contribution to runtime will be too small compared to the rest of the code (signal/noise).

sure, but I would argue that we should definitely not use something with UB

Benno Lossin said:

Jens Korinth said:

Benno Lossin said:

Jens Korinth said:

We're talking about milli- and microseconds here in a completely artificial example; in any real world scenario, say insmodding a .ko or something, it seems extremely unlikely that the runtime contribution of this code could even be measured with any degree of confidence?

If you can't even measure the difference, why do you even want to optimize this piece of code?

Well, there's a difference between things you cannot measure, and things that do not have impact. If this piece of code is used freely in a million different places it will have an impact on runtime performance. But you won't be able to detect it by looking at any one driver in isolation, because its contribution to runtime will be too small compared to the rest of the code (signal/noise).

sure, but I would argue that we should definitely not use something with UB

I agree. After all, we want to improve on the C code. :)

Alice Ryhl said:

I doubt that adding volatile will change the generated machine code in any real scenarios.

Yes, that is my impression, too. But I don't understand it well enough to be comfortable with any of the solutions. E.g., on my machine the AtomicBool versions now take ~450us, but the volatile only ~250us. No idea where that change came from.

My preferred solution would be to use AtomicBool with swap, because it would also fix the do_once/twice/thrice issue and really only execute once. But that takes ~4ms on my machine and I don't really understand why yet.
Using swap is definitely going to be more expensive since it introduces extra synchronization to avoid the do_twice case.
Usually in parallel programming area, the code with bugs runs faster.
@Boqun Feng That's very true, as can be seen by my naive implementation of the C code in the benchmark. :)

Jens Korinth said:

Alice Ryhl said:

I doubt that adding volatile will change the generated machine code in any real scenarios.

Yes, that is my impression, too. But I don't understand it well enough to be comfortable with any of the solutions. E.g., on my machine the AtomicBool versions now take ~450us, but the volatile only ~250us. No idea where that change came from.

I thought from the data you shared, AtomicBool version is also ~250us ish?

Benchmarking abool_relaxed: Warming up for 3.0000 scomplex 42 arguments are void
abool_relaxed           time:   [246.03 µs 246.93 µs 248.01 µs]
                        change: [-0.2734% +0.3719% +0.9428%] (p = 0.28 > 0.05)
                        No change in performance detected.
or by "AtomicBool versions" you mean the one with swap?

                        change: [-0.2734% +0.3719% +0.9428%] (p = 0.28 > 0.05)
                        No change in performance detected.

Yes - there were no changes in the code, but today it looks like this on my machine:

I'm on battery rn, will check again later when plugged in.
May be interesting to see what cargo bench reports on a different machines; I've pushed the commit with the swap to the git repo.

FWIW, you can do this:

diff --git a/benches/once_cmp.rs b/benches/once_cmp.rs
index 322e420..1bfc9cd 100644
--- a/benches/once_cmp.rs
+++ b/benches/once_cmp.rs
@@ -124,7 +124,7 @@ macro_rules! do_once_lite_if_abool_swap {
     ($cond:expr, $e:expr) => {
         #[link_section = ".data.once"]
         static ALREADY_DONE: AtomicBool = AtomicBool::new(false);
-        if $cond && !ALREADY_DONE.swap(true, Ordering::Relaxed) {
+        if $cond && !ALREADY_DONE.load(Ordering::Relaxed) && !ALREADY_DONE.swap(true, Ordering::Relaxed) {
             $e;
         }
     }

to improve the hotpath, because you don't need to xchg() each time after you set the flag.
And I didn't observe performance gap between mut_volatile_bool and abool_relaxed on my machines (x86 and arm64).
